questions.prompt: Complete: "You ___ wear a seatbelt in the car. It's the law."
questions.explanation: A legal obligation is expressed with must (or have to): You must wear a seatbelt. "don't have to" means it's optional, "shouldn't" gives advice against, and "must to" is wrong because must is followed by the base verb with no to.

questions.prompt: Complete: "This is a secret. You ___ tell anyone."
questions.explanation: Telling the secret is forbidden, so we use mustn't: You mustn't tell anyone. "don't have to" would mean it's optional (the opposite), while "should" and "have to" suggest a good idea or an obligation to tell — not a prohibition.

questions.prompt: Complete: "It's a casual party, so you ___ wear a suit if you don't want to."
questions.explanation: "if you don't want to" shows the suit is optional, so we use don't have to: you don't have to wear a suit. "mustn't" would forbid it, and "must / have to" would make it obligatory — neither matches a free choice.

questions.prompt: Complete: "You look exhausted. You ___ see a doctor."
questions.explanation: This is friendly advice, so we use should + base verb: You should see a doctor. "should to" is wrong (no to after should), "mustn't" would forbid it, and "don't have to" says it's optional rather than recommended.

questions.prompt: Complete: "She ___ get up early because her job starts at seven."
questions.explanation: With she, have to becomes has to: She has to get up early. "have to" doesn't agree with she, "must to" adds a wrong to, and "having to" is not a finite verb form here.
